raw.headline: California SCR 82 adopted by Assembly committee, urging UC, CSU, and CCC to form a joint AI workgroup on instruction, integrity, and student privacy
raw.what_happened: The California Assembly Higher Education Committee adopted Senate Concurrent Resolution 82 (Niello) during the week of July 5–7, 2026, urging the leaders of the University of California, California State University, and California Community Colleges to convene a joint intersegmental workgroup to establish unified best practices for AI use in instruction, academic integrity, plagiarism mitigation, and student privacy. The CSU Board of Trustees included SCR 82 status in its July 2026 legislative tracking report, signaling active monitoring by at least one of the named systems.
raw.why_it_matters: UC has systemwide academic integrity guidance (UCEP Annual Reminder, September 2025) and responsible AI principles, but no documented position on intersegmental coordination with CSU or CCC on AI policy, and the baseline shows no field for a joint-system AI workgroup. SCR 82 names the UC President directly as a convening party. The CSU has already deployed ChatGPT Edu to 470,000 students under a $17 million contract nearing expiration in July 2026 and has published multiple equity-framed AI strategy documents this week; UC has no comparable systemwide enterprise AI platform or unified equity access posture to bring to a joint workgroup table.
raw.for_the_committee: Should UC proactively designate a systemwide representative and a negotiating position for the SCR 82 intersegmental workgroup before the Legislature expects a response, and what UC governance assets — the AI Council, the Steering Committee, UCEP guidance — would UC offer as its baseline contribution?

raw.headline: A major AI cheating incident at Brown and Brown's GAITL Committee final report together sharpen the stakes for UC's academic integrity posture
raw.what_happened: Two developments at Brown University converged this week. First, Inside Higher Ed reported July 8, 2026 that a Brown economics professor voided a midterm after suspecting widespread AI cheating, resulting in 19 failing grades and reigniting national debate over AI detection tools and take-home assessment design. Second, Brown's Generative AI in Teaching and Learning (GAITL) Committee released its final report July 8, 2026, recommending university-wide baseline GenAI rules with departmental flexibility, centralized enterprise GenAI tool provision, updated academic codes, and new faculty training programs.
raw.why_it_matters: UC has systemwide UCEP academic integrity guidance updated with GenAI provisions (September 2025) and campus-level policies at Berkeley, UCLA, UCSD, UCSF, and others, but the baseline shows no field for a UC systemwide position on AI detection tools — and Berkeley's guidance explicitly flags detection tools as 'increasingly less accurate' while stopping short of a systemwide prohibition. The Brown incident demonstrates that without a UC-level position on detection tool use and take-home assessment design, campuses will continue to handle large-scale AI cheating cases inconsistently, and the reputational and legal exposure falls to individual campuses. Brown's GAITL model — baseline rules plus departmental flexibility — is structurally similar to what the UC systemwide UCEP guidance attempts, but Brown has paired it with centralized tool provision that UC lacks.
raw.for_the_committee: Should the committee recommend that the UC AI Council take a formal position on AI detection tool use and take-home assessment standards — either adopting the Berkeley 'no endorsement' posture systemwide or establishing a different floor — before the fall 2026 term?

feed_sources.title: Brown Professor Suspects Majority of His Class Used AI to Cheat

experts.why: Professor and past Academic Senate Vice-Chair at UCSB; academic integrity and AI assessment are core to her Critical AI scholarship and her campus governance experience
Co-chaired the Risk and Transparency subcommittee of the prior UC AI Council; has written publicly on AI's effects on assessment
AI accountability scholar; detection tool bias and equity implications of mass-adjudication integrity cases are within her documented expertise

items.headline: UCSD Chancellor Khosla launches the Institute for Applied Health Intelligence, adding a new campus-level health AI hub days after co-chairing the UC AI Steering Committee
reasons: baseline_anchor field "has_health_ai_governance_forum" not present on ucop_systemwide.health_ai

raw.headline: UCSD Chancellor Khosla launches the Institute for Applied Health Intelligence, adding a new campus-level health AI hub days after co-chairing the UC AI Steering Committee
raw.what_happened: UC San Diego announced on July 6–10, 2026 the establishment of the Institute for Applied Health Intelligence, a multidisciplinary hub described as leveraging digital technology and AI to improve healthcare delivery and patient outcomes. Chancellor Pradeep Khosla — co-chair of the UC AI Steering Committee — provided the official statement. The announcement appeared in both the UCSD Today newsroom and regional press.
raw.why_it_matters: UC San Diego already operates the Joint Center for Health Innovation (JCHI) and has named health AI leadership (inaugural Chief Health AI Officer Karandeep Singh, subsequently succeeded by Alexander Khalessi as interim AVC for Health Sciences Innovation and AI). Adding the Institute for Applied Health Intelligence creates a third distinct health AI organizational unit at a single campus, while UC's systemwide Health AI Governance Forum (CDI2), chaired by Cora Han, is the designated cross-system coordination body. The baseline records no systemwide field tracking the proliferation of campus-level health AI institutes or their relationship to CDI2 governance authority. The committee co-chair is now an institutional stakeholder in a campus health AI enterprise that the committee may be asked to govern.
raw.for_the_committee: How should the committee manage the co-chair's dual role as a Steering Committee leader and the Chancellor sponsoring a new campus health AI institute — and does CDI2 need a clearer charter defining when new campus health AI bodies must coordinate at the systemwide level?

experts.why: Chief Health Data Officer and CDI2 Executive Director; directly responsible for the systemwide health AI coordination body whose authority is implicated
